sections.summary: Non-substantive document title: Standard for Dried Oregano (CXS 342-2021).
Non-substantive note stating the standard was adopted in 2021 and amended in 2022.

Records a 2022 amendment that replaces the non-retail container labelling provision with a requirement that such labelling be in accordance with CXS 346-2021 (General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods).

sections.section: The following amendments were made to the text of the standard following decisions taken at the forty-fifth session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.
sections.summary: Non-substantive amendment note stating that the listed amendments resulted from decisions at the 45th session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission in December 2022.

Applies the standard to dried leaves/flowers of oregano (as defined in Section 2) offered for direct consumption, as an ingredient, or for repackaging, and explicitly excludes dried oregano intended for industrial processing.

Defines dried oregano as product from leaves and flowering tops processed by cleaning, drying, rubbing and sifting, and specifies available styles (whole, crushed/rubbed, ground/powdered) with particle size for ground forms determined by contract between buyer and seller.

Defines dried oregano as the product from leaves and flowering tops of the species in Table 1 (Origanum spp. L., excluding Origanum majorana L., and Lippia spp. L. for Mexican oregano) processed by cleaning, drying, rubbing and sifting.
Permits three styles of dried oregano—whole; crushed/rubbed (coarse to fine); and ground/powdered—and states particle size for ground/powdered forms is a matter for contractual agreement.

sections.section: 2.2.1 Dried oregano may be offered in one of the following styles
sections.summary: Lists the allowed presentation styles for dried oregano: whole, crushed/rubbed, or ground/powdered.

sections.section: 2.2.2 The particle size of ground/powdered styles is determined by contractual agreement between buyer and seller
sections.summary: Specifies that the particle size of ground/powdered oregano is determined by contractual agreement between buyer and seller.

Sets essential composition and quality provisions: moisture not over 12%, characteristic odour/flavour/colour and freedom from foreign/musty odours, classification of whole and crushed/rubbed oregano into extra, class I and class II (class II as minimum if ungraded), and directs compliance with detailed physical and chemical requirements given in Table 2 and Table 3 (including tolerances for extraneous/foreign matter, insect counts, visible mould/damage, excreta, ash limits and minimum volatile oil content).
States composition is the product defined in Section 2 (i.e., dried leaves/flowering tops of specified oregano species).

Specifies quality factors: moisture ≤12%; characteristic odour, flavour and colour without mustiness; grading into extra, class I and class II (with class II as the minimum if ungraded); and that physical and chemical limits (e.g., extraneous/foreign matter tolerances, dead insects ≤3/100 g, live insects 0/100 g, visible mould limits, mammalian excreta, ash and volatile oil minima) are prescribed in Tables 2 and 3.
Sets a maximum moisture content of 12% for dried oregano in all styles (whole, crushed/rubbed, or ground/powdered).

Requires dried oregano to have a characteristic odor, flavor (related to carvacrol/thymol composition) and a characteristic color (pale greyish yellow-green to dark green), and to be free from foreign or musty odors/flavors.

Defines three classification grades for whole and crushed/rubbed oregano (Extra, Class/Grade I, Class/Grade II) with physical and chemical requirements in Tables 2 and 3, and states that unclassified/ungraded product must meet Class/Grade II minimum requirements.

Specifies physical requirements and allowed tolerances by grade (Extra, I, II and ground) in Table 2, including limits for extraneous matter (e.g., 0.5% max for Extra, 2% for I/II), foreign matter (0.1% max), tolerance for powder contamination, dead insects (max 3/100 g), visible mould/insect damage (whole only; e.g., 1% Extra, 3% I, 5% II), zero live insects, and maximum mammalian and other excreta (mg/kg) for whole oregano.
States that anticaking agents listed in Table 3 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) are acceptable for use in powdered forms of foods conforming to this standard.

Requires compliance with chemical requirements in Table 3 specifying maxima for total ash (e.g., Extra 9% to ground 12% dry basis), acid‑insoluble ash (e.g., 1.2% to 2.5% dry basis) and minimum volatile oil content (e.g., Extra ≥2.5 ml/100 g to ground ≥1.3 ml/100 g) by grade/style.
States that anticaking agents listed in Table 3 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) are acceptable for use in powdered forms of foods conforming to this standard.

Requires products to comply with Codex maximum levels for contaminants and toxins (CXS 193-1995), the Code of Practice to prevent/reduce pyrrolizidine alkaloid contamination (CXC 74-2014), and Codex pesticide maximum residue limits.

Recommends preparation and handling in accordance with Codex hygiene texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1-1969, Code for Low‑Moisture Foods CXC 75-2015 Annex III for spices/herbs) and requires compliance with any established microbiological criteria per CXG 21-1997.
Requires containers to be filled as practicable without impairing quality and to be consistent with a proper declaration of contents.

Mandates labelling per the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) with the product name ('dried oregano' or 'oregano' if not misleading), style/class/grade and optional scientific name, mandatory country of origin (harvest country/region/year optional), optional particle size, and non‑retail labelling per CXS 346-2021.
Specifies that compliance checking shall use methods of analysis and sampling in CXS 234-1999 relevant to this standard, and notes that a sampling plan is to be developed.
Lists bibliographic references for the Codex texts cited (additives, contaminants, hygiene, methods); this section is reference material rather than substantive requirements.
